title.en: Modal Verbs — May / Must / Want / Can / Shall

shortcut: Modal at position 2, infinitive at the END. The five modals: kunnen (can), willen (want), moeten (must / have to), mogen (may / allowed), zullen (will / shall). ⚠ Prohibition trap: English 'must not' = Dutch MAG NIET (NOT 'moet niet').
explanation: Modal verbs express capacity, ambition, obligation, permission, and futurity. Dutch has five. They share a structural pattern (modal at slot 2, infinitive at the end of the clause) but each carries a different precise meaning. The biggest trap for English speakers is the modal + niet combination — a word-for-word translation of 'must not' as 'moet niet' inverts the meaning.

patterns.rule: The five modals — what each one means
examples: Each modal expresses a specific shade of meaning. They are NOT interchangeable.

examples.caption: The five modals at a glance

patterns.rule: Conjugation tables — all five are slightly irregular

examples.caption: kunnen (can) — ⚠ 'jij kunt' OR 'jij kan' both fine, but 'Kun je…?' after the verb
willen (want) — same drop: 'Wil je…?' after the verb
moeten (must / have to)
mogen (may / allowed to)
zullen (will / shall)

patterns.rule: Modal sentence structure — modal at slot 2, infinitive at the END
examples: Every modal sentence: conjugated modal at position 2, infinitive at the very end, everything else stacked between.

examples.caption: KUNNEN — capacity. 'I can speak Dutch.'
WILLEN — ambition. 'I want to read a book.'
MOETEN — obligation. 'I have to get up at 8.'
MOGEN — permission. 'You may wait here.'
ZULLEN — futurity / promise. 'I will come on Thursday.'

patterns.rule: ⚠ THE BIG TRAP — modal + niet (must-not vs don't-have-to)
examples: This is the single biggest gotcha for English speakers. English 'must not' and 'don't have to' look similar to Dutch 'moet niet' and 'mag niet' — but the mapping is INVERTED.
A direct word-for-word translation of 'you must not smoke' → 'je moet niet roken' gives the WRONG meaning. 'Moet niet' actually means 'don't have to' — almost the opposite!

examples.caption: MAG NIET = 'not allowed' / 'must not' (strong prohibition)
MOET NIET = 'don't have to' / 'needn't' (lack of obligation)
english: (NOT 'you must not' — it means you have no obligation!)

examples: Why English speakers fall in: 'moet' looks and sounds like 'must', so the brain reaches for it when wanting to say 'must not'. But Dutch uses MAG NIET (literally 'may not') for prohibition.

examples.caption: The inverted English ↔ Dutch mapping

examples: 🚭 Real-world test — a 'No Smoking' sign in a café:

examples.caption: ✅ Correct Dutch for 'No smoking allowed'
examples.note: Literally 'in the café you may not smoke' — i.e. 'you're not allowed'.

examples.caption: ❌ What 'In het café moet je niet roken' actually means
examples.note: An English speaker reads this as 'you must not smoke' (prohibition). A Dutch speaker reads it as 'in the café you don't have to smoke' — meaningless on a No Smoking sign!

examples: Belgian Dutch has a special verb 'hoeven' that's used ONLY in negative contexts (with niet, geen, nooit). It's a more formal, clearer way to say 'don't have to'.

examples.caption: HOEVEN NIET = 'don't have to' (formal, unambiguous)
examples.note: 'Hoeven' requires 'te' before the infinitive — that's the only modal-like verb in Dutch that does.

examples: ↳ Use 'hoeven niet' when you want to be UNAMBIGUOUS about 'don't have to'. It can't be confused with 'mag niet' (prohibition).

patterns.rule: Modal + inversion — fronting a time keeps the structure
examples: When something fronts (time, place, etc), the MODAL takes slot 2. The infinitive STILL stays at the end.

examples.caption: Default — 'I want to work from home today'
Inverted — time fronted, modal anchors slot 2

examples: ↳ See [[inversion]] for the full V2 rule. The pattern stacks: inversion + modal-at-slot-2 + infinitive-at-end.

patterns.rule: Polite 'wil graag' — the Dutch 'would like to'
examples: English 'I'd like to ___' is a politeness softener. Dutch uses 'wil graag' for the same purpose.

examples.caption: Direct: 'I want to take French class'
Polite: 'I would like to take French class'

examples: ↳ Anywhere you'd say 'I'd like to' in English, say 'ik wil graag' in Dutch. Standard polite opener for any service / enrolment / shop interaction.

memory: 🧠 Five modals, five meanings: kunnen=capacity, willen=ambition, moeten=obligation, mogen=permission, zullen=futurity. Structure: modal at slot 2, infinitive at the END. ⚠ Burn this in: English 'must not' = Dutch MAG NIET (NOT 'moet niet'!). 'Moet niet' = 'don't have to'. The 'No Smoking' sign uses MAG NIET.

examples.nl: Ik wil graag een koffie, alstublieft.
examples.en: I would like a coffee, please. (willen + graag = polite 'I'd like')
In the mornings I have to get up early. (moeten — obligation)
May I disturb you for a moment? (mogen — permission)

examples.nl: In het café mag je niet roken.
examples.en: In the café you may not / must not smoke. (mag + niet = prohibition)
You DON'T HAVE TO do that. (moet + niet = LACK of obligation — NOT 'you mustn't'!)
I shall come on Thursday. (zullen — promise / futurity)

practice.nl: Hoe zeg je 'I can cook well' in het Nederlands?
practice.en: How do you say 'I can cook well' in Dutch?

practice.nl: Ik kan goed koken. Modaal werkwoord 'kan' op positie 2, infinitief 'koken' aan het einde.
practice.en: Ik kan goed koken. Modal at position 2, infinitive at the end.

practice.nl: Een bord in een café zegt 'No smoking'. Vertaal naar het Nederlands.
practice.en: A sign in a café says 'No smoking'. Translate to Dutch.

practice.nl: 'Je mag niet roken' (= je bent niet toegestaan om te roken). NIET 'je moet niet roken' — dat betekent 'je hoeft niet te roken' en is precies het tegenovergestelde van een verbod!
practice.en: 'Je mag niet roken' (= you're not allowed to smoke). NOT 'je moet niet roken' — that means 'you don't have to smoke', the exact OPPOSITE of a prohibition!

practice.nl: Wat is het verschil tussen 'je moet niet' en 'je hoeft niet'?
practice.en: What's the difference between 'je moet niet' and 'je hoeft niet'?

practice.nl: Beide betekenen 'you don't have to'. 'Hoeven' is formeler en duidelijker — het kan niet verward worden met een verbod. 'Hoeven' vereist altijd 'te' voor de infinitief: 'Je hoeft niet TE komen.'
practice.en: Both mean 'you don't have to'. 'Hoeven' is more formal and clearer — can't be confused with prohibition. 'Hoeven' always requires 'te' before the infinitive.

practice.nl: Hoe vraag je beleefd om een koffie?
practice.en: How do you politely ask for a coffee?

practice.nl: 'Ik wil graag een koffie, alstublieft.' Of: 'Mag ik een koffie, alstublieft?' (mag = polite permission request.)
practice.en: 'Ik wil graag een koffie, alstublieft.' Or: 'Mag ik een koffie, alstublieft?'
